explanation: The statement specifies that 'prop_0' occurs either after 'prop_1', or occurs by 'prop_2'. In LTL, 'after prop_1' for 'prop_0' means that 'prop_0' must not hold until 'prop_1' holds, after which 'prop_0' must occur. 'By prop_2' for 'prop_0' means that 'prop_0' must hold at or before 'prop_2' (i.e., 'prop_0' must occur eventually and not later than 'prop_2'). All together, the formula states that either all occurrences of 'prop_0' are after 'prop_1' (i.e., 'prop_0' cannot occur before 'prop_1', but after 'prop_1', 'prop_0' can happen anytime), or 'prop_0' happens at or before 'prop_2'.
output_LTL: ((!(patientBreathingRequest | apnea)) U (breathingCycleDone)) -> F (patientBreathingRequest | apnea) | (F ((patientBreathingRequest | apnea) & (!(expiratoryPhase & patientBreathingRequest)) U (patientBreathingRequest | apnea)) | F ((expiratoryPhase & patientBreathingRequest) & (!(patientBreathingRequest | apnea)) U (expiratoryPhase & patientBreathingRequest)))

explanation: The requirement specifies that the initiation of a new breathing cycle should happen either whenever prop_0 occurs or whenever prop_1 occurs. Assuming the initiation of the cycle is abstracted as an event (let's call it 'init'), the LTL formula expresses that globally, whenever prop_0 or prop_1 becomes true, the initiation event must occur. Since 'init' is not explicitly listed as an atomic proposition, we can just express that whenever prop_0 or prop_1, then the condition for initiation is satisfied (represented by those propositions themselves).

The sentence requires that, in 'PCV mode', prop_0 should happen either after prop_1, or at least by the time prop_2 occurs. We interpret this as: (1) If prop_1 happens, then prop_0 must not occur before it -- prop_1 occurs before or at the same time as prop_0. (2) If prop_1 has not happened yet, then prop_0 must occur by the time prop_2 happens — that is, at or before the first occurrence of prop_2. This can be expressed as: Either (until prop_1 occurs, prop_0 does not occur) and then, after prop_1, prop_0 must occur; or, if prop_1 never occurs, then prop_0 must occur by the time (or at) prop_2 occurs. In LTL, this uses the 'until' and 'release' operators.
output_LTL: ((!(patientBreathingRequest | apnea | breathingTimerReset)) U (breathingCycleDone)) -> F ((breathingCycleDone) & F (patientBreathingRequest | apnea | breathingTimerReset)) | ((!(breathingCycleDone)) U (patientBreathingRequest & expiratoryPhase)) -> F[0:1] ((patientBreathingRequest | apnea | breathingTimerReset) | (patientBreathingRequest & expiratoryPhase))

explanation: The sentence requires that in PCV mode, prop_2 must occur either after prop_0 has occurred, or due to prop_1. This can be interpreted as: whenever prop_2 occurs, it is either caused by the previous occurrence of prop_0 (i.e., in the past there was a prop_0), or triggered by prop_1. Since LTL only supports future modalities, we can enforce: whenever prop_2 holds, either prop_1 also holds now, or, sometime before now, prop_0 has held. In pure LTL, we commonly express 'prop_2 only if prop_1 OR (previously prop_0)' as: G( prop_2 -> (F prop_0 U prop_2 | prop_1)). Alternatively, as we cannot refer to the past, we interpret as 'prop_2 must be immediately preceeded by prop_0, or coincide with prop_1'. In LTL, it is common to encode this as: G( prop_2 -> X((!prop_2) U (prop_0 | prop_1)) ), but with our restrictions, a simple interpretation is: whenever prop_2 occurs, prop_1 holds now, or previously prop_0 held. Thus, a standard future-only LTL expression is: G( prop_2 -> ( ( !prop_0 ) U prop_2 -> prop_1 ) )., but to stay in pure future, restrict to 'every prop_2 is immediately after prop_0, or at a time when prop_1 holds'. Adding: G( prop_2 -> ( X^-1 prop_0 | prop_1 ) ) is not possible, so semantically, prop_2 can only arise if prop_1 holds or after prop_0 happened.
output_LTL: G( (breathingCycleDone | (expiratoryPhase & patientBreathingRequest)) -> ( (expiratoryPhase & patientBreathingRequest) | F ( (breathingCycleDone) & X (breathingCycleDone | (expiratoryPhase & patientBreathingRequest)) ) ) )

explanation: The requirement states that in PCV mode, prop_0 should occur either after prop_1 or by prop_2. This means prop_0 does not occur before either prop_1 occurs or prop_2 occurs, and when one of them happens (the first of prop_1 or prop_2), then prop_0 must (eventually) occur, and not before. This is expressible as: If prop_2 happens first, then prop_0 occurs no later than prop_2 (i.e., at the same time or before or right after prop_2); if prop_1 happens first, then prop_0 occurs after prop_1. This is best captured by: Every occurrence of prop_0 is preceded by prop_1, or happens no later than prop_2 (i.e., not after prop_2).
output_LTL: G( (F (breathingCycleDone) -> F((breathingCycleDone) & X F (patientBreathingRequest | apnea | breathingCycleStart))) | (F (patientBreathingRequest & expiratoryPhase) -> F((patientBreathingRequest | apnea | breathingCycleStart) & F (patientBreathingRequest & expiratoryPhase))) )

explanation: This sentence requires that in PCV mode, prop_0 should happen either after prop_1, or by (at the latest) the time of prop_2. In LTL, this means that if prop_2 happens before prop_1, then prop_0 must at least happen when prop_2 occurs; if prop_1 happens first, then prop_0 happens some time after that. This can be captured under the interpretation: 'prop_0 must occur after prop_1, or at the latest when prop_2 occurs.' This can be formalized as: either (1) prop_1 happens at some point, and after that, eventually prop_0 happens before prop_2, or (2) prop_2 happens and prop_0 happens at the same time.

The requirement says that prop_0 must be initiated either after prop_1 has occurred, or after prop_2 has occurred. In LTL, we formalize 'after' as: prop_0 does not occur until either prop_1 or prop_2 occurs, and further, prop_0 must be preceded by one of them. So, whenever prop_0 occurs, it must have been preceded (at some strictly earlier time) by either prop_1 or prop_2.

The sentence indicates that 'prop_0' must happen either after 'prop_1' (i.e., prop_0 can't happen before prop_1), or by (at or before) 'prop_2' (i.e., prop_0 must happen at or before prop_2 happens). In LTL, 'after prop_1' can be captured as: 'if not prop_1 has happened, then prop_0 can't happen yet'. 'by prop_2' can be captured as: 'prop_2 happens, and until then, prop_0 may or may not happen, but if prop_2 happens, then prop_0 must happen at or before that'. Combining: globally, if prop_0 happens, then (prop_1 has already happened) or (prop_2 hasn't happened yet). Formally: (!prop_0 U prop_1) | (F[0:1] (prop_2 & (!prop_0 U prop_2))). In a more standard shape: G( prop_0 -> (F prop_1) | (F[0:1] prop_2) ).

Past-time operators like X^-1 are not allowed in standard LTL, so we must rewrite the formula using only the allowed operators. The phrase "prop_2 either after a prop_0, or by prop_1" requires that whenever prop_2 occurs, it is either simultaneously with prop_1 or preceded (eventually in the past) by prop_0, and no 'by' prop_1 was needed for prop_2 to happen. Since LTL lacks true past-time operators, the typical future-time encoding for 'p after q' is that 'q' must happen at some point before 'p', and between 'q' and 'p', 'p' does not occur. In LTL, for every moment when prop_2 happens and prop_1 does not hold, it must be the case that prop_0 has occurred before (with no prop_2 since then). This is encoded as: whenever prop_2 and not prop_1 occurs, then before that (in the past) there was a prop_0 and before that no prop_2, which we encode with releases: (!prop_2) R prop_0. Since we cannot have the actual past, we encode: globally, if prop_2 and not prop_1, then historically, prop_0 has happened and no prop_2 since then. In future LTL, this can be stated as: it is not possible to have prop_2 (without prop_1) unless prop_0 was seen before (so, globally, if prop_2 and not prop_1, then previously we had not had prop_2 since the last prop_0). This can be encoded by saying: always, if prop_2 and not prop_1, then previously it was the case that not prop_2 since prop_0 (i.e., (!prop_2) R prop_0).
output_LTL: G((((breathingCycleDone | (patientBreathingRequest & expiratoryPhase)) -> breathingCycleStart) & !(patientBreathingRequest & expiratoryPhase)) -> ((!((breathingCycleDone | (patientBreathingRequest & expiratoryPhase)) -> breathingCycleStart)) R (breathingCycleDone)))

explanation: The statement means that 'prop_0' occurs either after 'prop_1', or 'by' (no later than) when 'prop_2' occurs. That is, either 'prop_1' happens and then eventually 'prop_0' happens after that, or 'prop_0' occurs at or before 'prop_2'. Thus, this is captured as: '(prop_1 -> F(prop_1 & F prop_0)) | (F(prop_2 & G(!prop_0 U prop_2)))'. However, for pure LTL without explicit time references, we can rephrase as: (If prop_1 occurs, then after that, prop_0 must occur) or (prop_0 must occur no later than prop_2). In LTL, this becomes: (G(prop_1 -> F(prop_0))) | F(prop_2 & (!prop_0 U prop_2)).
output_LTL: (G ((breathingCycleDone) -> F (patientBreathingRequest | apnea))) | F ((patientBreathingRequest & expiratoryPhase) & ((!(patientBreathingRequest | apnea)) U (patientBreathingRequest & expiratoryPhase)))
